question: Implement and explain the Repository Pattern in Flutter.
answer:
The Repository Pattern abstracts data access behind an interface. The rest of the app works with the interface — it doesn't care if data comes from an API, a database, or a cache.

Benefits:
• Swap implementations without touching business logic
• Inject mocks in tests
• Centralize caching and offline logic
• Single source of truth for data

Pattern: Domain defines abstract interface → Data layer provides concrete implementation.

question: Explain common GoF design patterns in Flutter: Singleton, Factory, Observer, Strategy.
answer:
Design Patterns in Flutter context:

Singleton — single instance throughout app life (service locator, config)
Factory — create objects via a method that can return subtypes (platform-specific services)
Observer — publish/subscribe (ChangeNotifier, Streams are the pattern!)
Strategy — plug-in interchangeable algorithms at runtime
Builder — build complex objects step-by-step
Command — encapsulate operations as objects (Actions, undo/redo)

question: Explain Dependency Injection in Flutter. Compare get_it vs injectable vs Riverpod.
answer:
Dependency Injection (DI) provides dependencies to a class from outside rather than creating them internally — enabling testability, flexibility, and loose coupling.

Approaches in Flutter:

get_it — service locator (global registry):
• Simple, no code generation
• Not compile-time safe (runtime lookup)
• Great for simple to medium apps

injectable (wraps get_it):
• Adds code generation with annotations
• @injectable, @singleton, @lazySingleton, @module
• Compile-time safe via generated code

Riverpod:
• First-class DI built into state management
• Compile-time safe, auto-dispose, testable
• Best for new Flutter apps

question: How do you apply the Adapter pattern to wrap an inconsistent third-party API?
answer:
An Adapter wraps a class with one interface and exposes it through another. Useful when you depend on a third-party library or legacy API whose surface doesn't match the contract the rest of your app expects — and you don't control either side.

When to reach for it:
• Two analytics SDKs you want to behind a single `Analytics` contract
• A payment SDK with weird typed callbacks; you want a clean `Future<Result> charge(...)` API
• A platform SDK with platform-specific data types — wrap them into your domain types

Forms in Dart:
• Class adapter — extends both your interface and the foreign class. Rare; Dart's no multiple inheritance limits this.
• Object adapter (typical) — implements your interface, holds the foreign object as a field, and translates calls

The adapter is the "anti-corruption layer". It keeps the foreign vocabulary out of your domain code; if the third party releases a breaking v2, only the adapter changes.

question: How do you implement the Decorator pattern in Dart, and when do mixins or extensions cover the same need?
answer:
Decorator wraps an object to add behavior without changing the wrapped object's class. The wrapper implements the same interface and forwards calls to the inner instance, sandwiching extra logic around them.

Classic uses:
• Logging / metrics around a repository or HTTP client
• Caching layer in front of a network client
• Retry / circuit-breaker around any service
• Authorization checks in front of a domain service

Why you'd pick it over inheritance:
• Cross-cutting concerns can be composed (Auth + Logging + Cache stacked)
• You can swap or remove a decorator without touching the wrapped class
• You can decorate at runtime (different decorators per environment / per feature flag)

Dart-specific alternatives:
• Mixin — folds behavior into the class itself; can't be composed at runtime, but cleaner when the behavior is intrinsic
• Extension — adds methods without changing the type. Doesn't work for overriding interface methods. Good for static helpers, bad for cross-cutting concerns that need polymorphism.
• Decorator class — when the behavior must be optional, runtime-composable, and visible through the same interface, this is still the right tool

question: How do you implement the Command pattern with undo/redo support?
answer:
A Command encapsulates an operation as an object with an `execute()` method (and optionally an `undo()`). Storing commands instead of just calling functions buys you:

• Undo/redo stacks — push executed commands onto a history; pop and call undo()
• Macro-recording — replay a sequence of commands later, in tests, or for telemetry
• Decoupling sender from receiver — a button doesn't know what "delete user" does, only that it triggers a Command bound to its handler
• Auditing / logging — log every command executed; great for compliance trails

When NOT to:
• Trivial UI actions — wrapping every button in a Command is overkill
• Only when you need replay, undo, or composition

Dart-friendly shape:
• Sealed class hierarchy of commands
• `CommandRunner` keeps undo and redo stacks
• Each command captures the BEFORE state it needs to restore — never re-derive in undo()

question: How does the State pattern compare with Dart 3 sealed classes?
answer:
Both express "this entity behaves differently in each of N modes". They live at different levels of formality and trade off in interesting ways.

State pattern (classic GoF):
• An object delegates behavior to a `State` field that implements an interface
• The state object can swap itself out (`context.state = NextState()`)
• Each state knows which states are reachable from it — transitions live in code, not in a switch table
• Best when each state has rich, nontrivial behavior of its own

Sealed class + pattern matching (Dart 3):
• A `sealed` parent with concrete subtypes; the surrounding code switches on which subtype is current
• Compiler checks exhaustiveness — adding a new state forces every switch to handle it
• States become value objects; transitions are usually expressed in the surrounding code (a Notifier/Cubit/Bloc)
• Best for state that's mostly data with little intrinsic behavior

Which to pick:
• Many transitions, each state has rich behavior, want behavior co-located with state → State pattern
• Mostly inert data shapes that drive UI/logic externally → sealed classes
• In Flutter app code with Riverpod/Bloc/Cubit, sealed classes win 9 times out of 10 because the state itself is just data and the Notifier holds the logic.

question: What is the BLoC pattern, and how does it differ from the Observer pattern in general?
answer:
BLoC (Business Logic Component) is a specific Flutter incarnation of the broader Observer pattern, with three opinionated rules:

• Inputs are events — discrete, named, immutable. UI dispatches them.
• Outputs are states — also immutable. UI rebuilds from them.
• In between is a single class that maps events to states, often via stream transformations

Observer pattern in general:
• Some Subject notifies any number of Observers when something changes
• Observers can themselves be anywhere — UI, services, other components
• ChangeNotifier, Stream, ValueNotifier are all Observer implementations

What BLoC adds on top:
• A name and shape for inputs (events) and outputs (states)
• Cancellation of in-flight work when a new event supersedes (e.g. switchMap on search)
• Replayability — the event log can be recorded and replayed for tests, time-travel debugging
• A clear test surface — give the BLoC events, expect states; no widget test needed

When Observer is enough:
• Simple, local UI state (a checkbox, a tab index) — ChangeNotifier or ValueNotifier is plenty
• You don't need event/state history

When BLoC pays off:
• Complex screens with many inputs and async loading flows
• You want to test business logic without rendering UI
• You need debounce / switchMap-style cancellation in business logic, not in widgets

question: When should you use the Service Locator pattern vs constructor injection?
answer:
Both deliver dependencies to the code that uses them, but they trade off in different directions.

Service Locator (get_it / GetIt):
• A global registry holds all your services
• Code looks them up by type at the call site: `final repo = sl<UserRepository>()`
• Pros: zero plumbing, accessible anywhere (tap handlers, isolates, free functions)
• Cons: dependencies are HIDDEN at the call site (you must read the code to know what it needs); harder to mock by-test (must reset the global registry); ordering of registrations matters

Constructor Injection (Riverpod / Provider, or hand-rolled):
• Each class declares its dependencies as constructor args
• Pros: dependencies are EXPLICIT (the constructor signature is the contract); easy to test (pass mocks directly); compile-time safe
• Cons: more plumbing — you have to thread dependencies through constructors; needs a composition root or a DI framework to wire it up

Rule of thumb in Flutter:
• New code, especially anything testable: Riverpod / explicit constructor injection — modern and compile-time safe
• Cross-cutting concerns that genuinely need to be reachable from everywhere (logger, analytics): a service locator is fine, but ONLY for those
• Legacy code or teams that prefer simplicity: get_it works perfectly; just be disciplined about test setup

Mixing the two is fine in practice: Riverpod for application services and view models; get_it for plugins or true singletons that don't fit Riverpod's lifecycle.
